text:
FY26 performance snapshot (vs YoY; guidance vs actual)
Balance-sheet growth: Business +14.95% YoY; gross advances +19.44%; deposits +11.59%.
Deposit mix & franchise: CASA +12.46% (Savings +11.78%, Current +16.77%); CASA ratio 38.65% (up 75 bps YoY), above guidance (37–38%).
Advance mix (pivot to RAM): RAM >24% YoY; Retail >26%, Agri ~26%, MSME ~19%. Retail highlights: housing +~19%, car loans +~71%.
Asset quality: GNPA 2.17% (improved 52 bps YoY), NNPA 0.27% (improved 23 bps). PCR 97.79% (up 110 bps); PCR ex-TWO 87.66% (up 571 bps).
Profitability: FY26 operating profit ₹6,429cr (+6.49%); net profit ₹2,768cr (+13.21%). Q4 net profit ₹801cr (+22%).
Execution vs FY26 guidance (select):
Deposits 11.59% (10–12%); Credit 19.44% (12–15%)
RAM 65% (61–63%); CD ratio 80.21% (75–77%)
Credit cost 0.62% (<1%); Global NIM 3.03% (2.8–2.9%); Domestic NIM 3.23%
Slippage ratio 0.78% (1–1.25%); Recovery/upgradation ₹2,944cr (₹2,200–2,700cr)
Capital & shareholder return: CRAR 18.61%, Tier-1 16.59%. Dividend 4.40% (₹0.44/share); payout ~20% (subject to AGM).
FY27 management guidance and what it implies

FY27 guidance (management):

Deposits 10–12%; Credit 12–14%
CASA 37–38%; RAM 62–65%
CD ratio 80–82%
Credit cost <0.75%
Global NIM 2.8–2.9%
GNPA <2%, NNPA <0.2%
Slippage ratio <1%
Recovery/upgradation ₹2,000–2,500cr

Key interpretation from management (re: conservative guidance / geopolitics):

CEO explicitly rejected that guidance was lowered due to West Asia/geopolitical stress: “there is no such thing that because of this temporary crisis, we have shifted our guidance downwards.”
Pattern claimed: guidance held steady historically while outcomes beat guidance.
Asset quality, stress monitoring, and provisioning buffers
SMA trends: Management emphasized SMA control; disclosed SMA (≥₹1cr) totals at ~0.45% of standard advances (SMA0/1/2 combined). CEO attributed SMA bucket movement to the “February effect” (28 days) causing apparent shift from SMA2 to SMA1 rather than fresh stress: “overall SMA remains the same, only bucket shifting is happening.”
Geopolitical impact (MSME): No material stress observed yet: “we have not yet seen any major impact”; MSME slippages and SMA trend said to be consistent with prior quarters.
Guarantee coverage: “around 40% of our advances are covered under CGTMSE.”
ECL / overlays / contingency: Significant management focus on building buffers:
ECL provision buffer ₹1,038cr (up from ₹720cr prior quarter)
Contingency provision ₹341cr
COVID provision ₹530cr still available
CEO: “all three put together today, we are holding around ₹1,900 crore of additional provision as a buffer towards our ECL framework.”
Margin, pricing discipline, and corporate pipeline
Pricing discipline as a key lever: On corporate growth, CEO stressed refusing low-yield deals: “we do not want credit growth in the Corporate segment at the cost of margins.”
Corporate pipeline: ~₹14,000cr corporate pipeline; some sanctioned but not disbursed due to pricing.
Sectoral demand cited (corporate): Renewables, data centers, smart metering; roads beginning to show some traction.
Rate benchmarking: “More than 65% of our portfolios linked to Repo.” Retail/MSME are largely repo-linked; some corporate short-term also linked to repo (even if sanctioned at MCLR).
NIM outlook tension: Despite FY26 global NIM 3.03%, FY27 guide is 2.8–2.9%, implying management expects margin normalization (no further explanation beyond guidance).
Deposits: strategy shifts with higher CD ratio
With CD ratio now ~80%, management states deposit focus increased: “Earlier, we were not focusing on deposits because our CD ratio was quite low. Now we have reached… around 80%… That is the reason we focused more on deposits.”
Still emphasizes deposit quality:
Focus on retail term deposits + CASA, not bulk: “our focus… is more on the Retail franchise than bulk deposits.”
Bulk deposits are used but within an internally monitored cap: “we continue monitor and maintain our bulk deposit within a defined ratio.”
Digital transformation and operating model modernization (Project Parivartan)

Delivered / in-flight operating upgrades:

Project Parivartan: 31 customer journeys completed across segments; digital business >₹25,000cr in FY26 (₹11,000cr advances; ₹14,000cr liabilities).
Mobile banking adoption: Registered users 82 lakh → 153 lakh (3 years); active users 14 lakh → 70 lakh; active/registered ratio 17% → 46%.
Tab-based onboarding: All branches equipped; >10 lakh accounts opened via tab banking in FY26; excluding BSBD/PMJDY, 66% of accounts opened via tab.
WhatsApp banking: >20 lakh customers onboarded; 49 services in 14 languages.
Digital origination: “More than 2,50,000 customers have been given loan digitally” in FY26; >50% of FD creation and loan-against-FD processed digitally; >50% of car loans via STP journey.
Technology stack upgrades implemented: New treasury solution Murex; data center consolidation completed (Kolkata); API gateway live; ALM TPM automation; application performance monitoring; Mule account monitoring (UCO VIGILPLUS) and integration with MuleHunter/IPC; real-time feedback loops via SMS/QR/website/WhatsApp.

FY27 planned initiatives (notable):

Omnichannel experience (explicitly aimed at “new generation” customers)
Cash management services, supply chain finance, RPA, digital marketing solutions
Forex & prepaid cards; document management system; e-note (paperless)
Cybersecurity: identity access management, centralized log management, vault/DR improvements
Operational centers: CASA back office, centralized forex processing center
Call center to be converted into a profit center; Parivartan Phase 2 launched
Centralized monitoring for loans sanctioned through ED CAM portal
Transformation Management vertical upgraded (GM-level leadership)
Cost efficiency, fee income, productivity
Cost-to-income: 52.66%, improved 581 bps YoY. Management attributes improvements to (i) tighter controllable cost budgeting/monitoring and (ii) higher fee income.
Fee-based income: ₹516cr vs ₹389cr (+32% YoY).
Productivity: Business per employee ₹28cr (vs ₹24cr); business per branch ₹173cr (vs ₹155cr).
Wage/employee cost volatility (quarterly): Explained as prior-year PLI provisioning; not reversed and some provision carried forward (~₹160cr).
Treasury / AFS mark-to-market: what happened and sensitivity
CEO described FY26 Q4 yield spike (>7%) driving MTM losses on AFS/HFT:
Treasury P&L was ₹-16cr in Q4 (vs ~₹130cr profit in prior quarter); AFS MTM impact ~₹135cr.
AFS reserves: -₹140cr (negative).
Reversal potential already visible with yields easing: “some of the… losses… booked in March, they have already been reversed if we take MTM today.”
Outlook remains conditional: “subject to the global stability in the environment.”
Other portfolio details & risk signals
Retail ‘other’ bucket: Mainly gold loans and staff loans; gold loans in retail ~₹5,400cr and drive most of the growth in “others.”
Total gold loans: Retail ₹5,400cr + Agri ₹12,200cr = ~₹18,000cr; yield estimated ~8.5–9% (management caveat: not exact).
Personal loan NPAs: Slight uptick (₹31cr → ₹39cr) on a ₹2,700cr book; management downplayed: “not substantial… hardly 1.43%.”
ROA aspiration: Current trajectory cited; CEO expects “by end of next financial year, we should be nearing 0.95 to 1% ROA levels.”
Capital actions (QIP) and timing
QIP is not imminent: management will seek shareholder approval at AGM and then act “at the right opportune time, when the market also supports”; “not… immediate, this quarter.”
